passage: A 2023 oceanographic study measured sea surface temperature (SST) increases at five North Atlantic monitoring stations over a 30-year period. Station A showed an increase of 0.4°C; Station B, 0.6°C; Station C, 0.9°C; Station D, 1.3°C; and Station E, 1.8°C. Researchers noted that Stations C, D, and E were located within 100 miles of major shipping lanes, while Stations A and B were situated in remote, low-traffic zones.
question: A researcher claims that proximity to shipping lanes is associated with greater sea surface temperature increases. Which data from the study most directly supports this claim?

choices.A: Station A and Station B, both in remote zones, showed the two smallest temperature increases (0.4°C and 0.6°C).
choices.B: Station E showed the largest temperature increase of any station in the study, at 1.8°C.
choices.C: The three stations near shipping lanes (C, D, E) showed increases of 0.9°C, 1.3°C, and 1.8°C, while the two remote stations (A, B) showed increases of only 0.4°C and 0.6°C.
choices.D: The average temperature increase across all five stations was approximately 1.0°C over 30 years.

explanation: The researcher's claim requires showing a consistent pattern linking proximity to shipping lanes with higher temperature increases. C provides both groups' data in parallel—all three near-lane stations had higher increases (0.9–1.8°C) than both remote stations (0.4–0.6°C)—which is the most direct and complete support. A shows only the remote stations and establishes no comparison with the near-lane group. B cites only the single highest data point without establishing a group-level pattern. D presents an overall average that obscures the between-group difference relevant to the claim.
